TextENGNew: The smoking area was hazy with cigarette smoke.
The expensive air purifier, treated as a high-end asset, did nothing but emit an annoying crackling sound -- it didn't seem to be helping at all.
Why did smokers have to be crammed into this sunless dead end of a corridor?
...I seemed to recall hearing that tobacco tax revenue accounted for roughly a tenth of local government income...
We were the top taxpayers propping up the local municipality, so couldn't we get a little better treatment...?
``......Hmm, why would you discard the 5-man here?@k@vS01/19/123100002. You're just narrowing your waits, aren't you?``
changeReason: New uses proper mahjong terminology: 'narrowing your waits' (待ち) instead of the vague 'decreasing your options', which more accurately conveys the technical mahjong discussion.

TextENGNew: A younger colleague was poring over the 'Next Move' column in a mahjong magazine.
changeReason: Old misidentifies 次の一手 as the magazine's name while JPN describes it as a column within a mahjong magazine; minor structural error.

TextENGNew: ``Cutting the 5-man doesn't break your tenpai.``
``If you're banking on a haitei draw, wouldn't you have a better chance with a two-sided wait?``

``Kuma-chan, look at the discard pond.@k@vS01/11/120700517. Everyone's already thrown a 5-man, so it's a safe tile.@k@vS01/11/120700518. In the endgame, shape your hand for safety, then wait for the draw -- that's the smart play.``
The kid groaned, stubbed out his cigarette, and immediately pulled out another.
``...I just can't accept it...@k@vS01/19/123100006. Why would you deliberately reduce your own winning chances?``
``By the way, you can't declare ron on the haitei tile even if it's your winning tile.``
``What?! Why not?!``
Just then, a voice came from down the corridor.
``Ooishi-san, are you here?@k@vS01/00/dek02002. You have an outside call from a civilian.``
``Oh, would you look at that. Well then, excuse me a moment!``
``W-Why can't you call ron on it?!@k@vS01/19/123100009. Wait, hold on, Ooishi-san!``
The man across from Ooishi's desk was waving the phone receiver.
``Outside line.@k@vS01/00/dek02004. From a payphone.``
``Oh, thanks very much!@k@vS01/11/120700522. ......Sorry to keep you waiting -- this is Ooishi.@k@vS01/11/120700523. Hello?``
``Well, if it isn't Maebara-san.@k@vS01/11/120700525. Why, good evening...!``
From the tone of Maebara-kun's voice alone, I could tell immediately that something was very wrong.
...This was the first time Maebara-kun had ever called me.
And he was calling from a payphone.
``Please calm down.@k@vS01/11/120700527. What happened?``
The voice on the other end was chaotic, completely stripped of composure.
After confirming no one else could hear, I cupped the receiver and spoke in a low voice, urging him on.
``What happened?!``
``...Calm down, Maebara-san!@k@vS01/11/120700530. I'll send the nearest officers to you right away.@k@vS01/11/120700531. I'm heading there myself too!``
``......Ah, um...... that's probably...... no use.``
His voice held both terror and a strange resignation...
changeReason: Old describes the voice quality ('quivering and hoarse') but JPN 怯えと達観 describes the emotional content: fear and resignation; new translation captures this distinction.

TextENGNew: ......Had he already been surrounded, even as he was making this call?!
``Maebara-san, you're calling from a payphone, correct?@k@vS01/11/120700533. Which payphone are you at?!``
There was no background noise at all beyond Maebara-kun's voice.
He had to be inside a phone booth.
I scrawled a note and thrust it at the colleague sitting across from me.
(Hinamizawa, phone booth!!)
My colleague grasped the situation immediately and began frantically placing internal calls.
``...Calm down, Maebara-san!@k@vS01/11/120700535. What is your situation right now...?!``
I knew it was bad practice to press someone already in a panic... but this wasn't that kind of case.
...Maebara-kun hadn't escaped danger and then called me afterward.
......He was in the middle of some crisis, right this moment...!
But... no matter how much I pressed, it would only make him more frantic.
Maebara-kun hadn't called just to ask for help.
...There was something more he was trying to convey.
And whatever that something was,@k ......he was certain that if this chance slipped by, he would never be able to communicate it again...!
changeReason: Old attributes the certainty to Ooishi ('I was certain') but the Japanese indicates it's Keiichi's conviction (確信に基づいている); new correctly attributes it to Keiichi ('he was certain').

TextENGNew: My colleague slid a note back to me.
(There's only one phone booth in Hinamizawa.@k A patrol car is en route.@k Five minutes.)
``That's too long.@k@vS01/11/120700537. How many officers are in the patrol car?``
Not enough.
......If my suspicion was right... Maebara-kun was surrounded by multiple people.
And five minutes was far too long!
``Did you contact the local officer stationed in Hinamizawa?!``
``He's out on his routine patrol.@k@vS01/00/dek02007. No one's home; unreachable.``
``Damn it!!@k@vS01/11/120700540. Kuma-chan, bring the car around.``
``Roger!!!``
``Hello!@k@vS01/11/120700542. It's all right.@k@vS01/11/120700543. I can hear you clearly!``
Something was wrong with Maebara-kun...
That coughing didn't sound normal.
......Vomiting?
Or could it possibly be... blood?!
Had he already been attacked and injured?!

``Maebara-san, officers are heading to your location right now.@k@vS01/11/120700545. They'll be there in two or three minutes -- please hold on!@k@vS01/11/120700546. Hello?!@k@vS01/11/120700547. Can you hear me?!@k@vS01/11/120700548. Maebara-san?!``
I could hear violent coughing from the other end of the line.
......The worst possible scenario crossed my mind.
``Maebara-san!!@k@vS01/11/120700550. Who is the culprit?!@k@vS01/11/120700551. How many are there?!``
``......I... at first... I thought the culprit was human... *gack*!!``
A sound somewhere between a cough and a retch.
``Are you all right?! Maebara-san!!``

"I thought the culprit was human... that there was no such thing as Oyashiro-sama's curse... that's what I believed...@k@vS01/01/120100818. Until just now.@k@vS01/01/120100819. ...But... I guess... after all... *hack hack*...!!"
A violent cough.@k Followed by vomiting.
``......But... I think... Oyashiro-sama... really does exist......@k@vS01/01/120100821. No -- he's real.@k@vS01/01/120100822. He's real, and he's right here......``
``Maebara-san, please, I beg you, calm down...``
``I've been thinking something's been off for a while now...@k@vS01/01/120100824. It's been following me this whole time...!!``

"No matter how far I run, no matter how far I run...!!!@k@vS01/01/120100826. It clings to me like a shadow!!@k@vS01/01/120100827. But slowly... bit by bit...@k@vS01/01/120100828. It creeps closer and closer to my back......"
``......Maebara-san... is it... right now... by any chance... behind you...?``
``Please, Maebara-san.@k@vS01/11/120700556. ......I understand you're frightened.@k@vS01/11/120700557. But please!!@k@vS01/11/120700558. ......Who is it... behind you...?!``
``You think I can just... look behind me...?@k@vS01/01/120100832. If I turn around... I'll... I'll...``
"I know you're scared!!@k@vS01/11/120700560. But please, tell me!!@k@vS01/11/120700561. Just turn around for a second, that's all!!@k@vS01/11/120700562. Behind you, Maebara-san... who is it?!?!"
Immediately after, I heard violent retching.@k Then some other, unpleasant sound.
``...Maebara-san,@k@vS01/11/120700564. ......you're not... by any chance...@k@vS01/11/120700565. ...clawing at your own throat... are you...?``
There was no answer.
...Just the sound of scratching... tearing...
There was a crash and clatter, as though something had been knocked over.
...Maebara-kun had probably dropped the receiver.
From the other end came groaning, retching, and that repeating... that terrible sound.

changeReason: New replaces the final もしもーしッ？！？！ ('Helloooo?!?!') with 'Maebara-san!!' which is not what the Japanese says; old translation preserves the drawn-out desperate 'hello'.

TextENGNew: I knew exactly how faint my voice must sound from a dropped receiver.
But... I couldn't help shouting.
Then, from the other end... I heard murmuring.
I couldn't make out what he was saying.
......From the cadence... was it a monologue?
Or was he... speaking to someone there?
It wasn't so much murmuring as... a monotone repetition, like a sutra.
I sharpened every nerve, straining to catch what he was saying...
What was he repeating......
What...... exactly......!!!
*Click!*
The line went dead with brutal abruptness.@k ......Had he run out of coins?!
changeReason: Old vaguely says 'use up his time' while JPN 10円が切れた specifically references running out of coins in a payphone; new preserves this cultural detail.

TextENGNew: It was a payphone, after all...!!
It was precisely because it cut off so suddenly.
...That his final words, ironically, came back to me with perfect clarity.
``Ooishi-san, the car's ready!!!@k@vS01/19/123100012. Ooishi-san...?!``

changeReason: Old phrasing 'It was... I'm sorry' could be misread as Ooishi apologizing; new uses quotation marks to clarify he's reporting what Keiichi was repeating: 'I'm sorry.'

TextENGNew: ``Ooishi-san......??``
That was what Maebara-kun had been repeating, over and over.
In that moment, I knew.
............There was no longer any need to rush.
......Just then, from beyond the open window... I heard the cry of the higurashi.
Kana kana kana kana kana......
changeReason: New uses 'Kana kana' which is the direct romanization of the Japanese カナカナ (the characteristic higurashi cicada cry), replacing the English approximation 'Kwee kwee'.

TextENGNew: They must have been calling this whole time.@k ...I just hadn't paid them any mind.
Why had they suddenly caught my attention now?
Kana kana kana kana kana......
changeReason: Same as above: new uses 'Kana kana' romanization of カナカナ instead of the English approximation 'Kwee kwee'.

TextENGNew: Were they trying to tell me something?
Only the higurashi knew.@k ......That's how it felt.
June, Showa 58.
changeReason: Old converts 昭和58年 to Western '1983' but new preserves the original Showa era dating 'Showa 58' used in the Japanese text, maintaining the formal Japanese document style.

TextENGNew: In the remote village of Hinamizawa within Shishibone City, a murder involving school students occurred.
changeReason: Old adds 'two' which isn't in this line and correctly notes 'female'; new drops both 'two' and the gender specification 女子 (female) from 女子生徒; neither fully matches the source.

TextENGNew: The suspect: Keiichi Maebara, a minor residing in Hinamizawa village (referred to as 'Juvenile A' in media reports).
changeReason: New includes key details from JPN: Hinamizawa village residence and the 'Juvenile A' media designation (少年A); old substitutes '(Aged 1x)' which is not in the source text.

TextENGNew: The suspect lured two female classmates (Rena Ryuugu and Mion Sonozaki) to his home and bludgeoned them to death with a metal bat.
The crime scene was the suspect's own room on the second floor of his house.
The room was drenched in a horrific amount of spattered blood, with clear signs of a struggle with the victims.
In addition to the crime scene itself, signs of disturbance were found in the entryway, living room, and kitchen.
In the entryway, the shoe rack and wall bore heavy impact marks.
These were determined to be from the murder weapon -- the bat. No blood traces were detected, suggesting the damage was inflicted prior to the killings.
There is a possibility that the suspect performed acts of intimidation to prevent the victims from fleeing.
changeReason: Old says 'overpowered' implying physical force, but JPN 威圧行為 means 'acts of intimidation'; new correctly translates this as intimidation rather than physical overpowering.

TextENGNew: In the living room, the carpet had been pulled up and thrown aside.
It is difficult to attribute this to a struggle with the victims; its purpose remains unknown.
In the kitchen, a garbage bag had been torn open and its contents scattered across the floor.
Refuse was strewn about the surrounding area, and handprints believed to belong to the suspect were discovered.
It is believed the suspect emptied the garbage for some reason and struck the contents with his bare hands.
The purpose of this remains unknown.
Additionally, a note affixed to the refrigerator read: 'Was there no needle?'
changeReason: Old translates 針がなかった？ as 'Was there a needle?' (positive) but the Japanese uses the negative なかった, meaning 'Was there no needle?'; new correctly preserves the negative form.

TextENGNew: The meaning is unclear. The garbage was searched as a precaution, but no needle was found.
While not disturbed, the garage shutter, which had been left open since the family moved in, was found closed. The suspect's fingerprints were detected on the shutter. The reason remains unknown.
changeReason: Old mistranslates 荒らされてはいなかったが ('while not disturbed') as 'the garage door was functional'; new correctly renders this. Also, new uses 'shutter' for シャッター which is more precise than 'door'.

TextENGNew: The suspect fled the crime scene, but was discovered collapsed inside a telephone booth by a patrolling officer from the Hinamizawa substation.

At the time of discovery, the suspect was unconscious and in critical condition. He was immediately transported to the village clinic for treatment, but never regained consciousness and died twenty-four hours later.
changeReason: Old says 'local hospital' but JPN 村内の診療所 means 'village clinic'; a 診療所 is a small clinic, not a hospital, and this distinction matters for understanding the limited medical resources available.

TextENGNew: The autopsy determined the direct cause of death to be hemorrhagic shock.
changeReason: Old says 'hypovolemic shock' but JPN 出血性ショック specifically means 'hemorrhagic shock' (from blood loss); new translation uses the more precise medical term matching the Japanese.

TextENGNew: It was concluded that the suspect had clawed open his own throat with his fingernails, and the resulting blood loss proved fatal.
Due to the striking resemblance to the bizarre death of Tomitake-san the previous week, the police opened an investigation treating the cases as potentially connected.
(However, at the strong request of local authorities, the investigation would be conducted confidentially.)
The abnormal manner of death raised suspicions of drug use, but as with the Tomitake case, no substances of any kind were detected.
Initially, the sheer inexplicability of it all led investigators to classify it as an impulsive, spontaneous crime.
changeReason: Old misses 当初は (initially), which is important context indicating this was only the preliminary assessment that was later revised.

TextENGNew: However, as accounts of the suspect's increasingly erratic behavior in the days leading up to the crime came to light, this assessment was revised.
Estrangement from his close-knit group of friends. Isolation. Incomprehensible words and actions.
Several days before the incident, the suspect had begun carrying a metal bat with him at all times.
Aggressive behavior and muttering to himself were frequently observed at school; classmates had personally witnessed some of it.
Two days before the crime, the suspect had made statements to his parents alluding to his own death.
Based on these circumstances, the police launched an investigation into the possibility that the crime was not spontaneous but premeditated, planned days in advance.
Subsequently, a handwritten note was discovered in the suspect's room.
The note consisted of two pieces torn from a B5 college-ruled notebook page, concealed behind a wall clock as though to hide it.
The contents of the note are as attached.
changeReason: Old contains the wall clock detail which belongs to the previous entry's Japanese text; this entry's JPN メモの内容は別添の通り means 'The contents of the note are as attached', which new correctly translates.

TextENGNew: The police regarded it as closely related to the incident.
changeReason: Old contains content ('contents as written in the appendix') that belongs to the previous entry; this entry's JPN is about police treating the note as closely related to the incident, which new correctly translates.

TextENGNew: The investigation shifted direction once again, on the possibility that the suspect had been caught up in some sort of incident.
changeReason: Old contains content from the previous entry; this entry's JPN describes the investigation pivoting based on the suspect possibly being caught up in a larger affair, which new correctly translates.

TextENGNew: However, no further leads were found, and the credibility of the note itself came into question.
changeReason: Old contains content from the previous entry; this entry's JPN describes the lack of leads and questioning of the note's credibility, which new correctly translates.

TextENGNew: Was this crime impulsive, or premeditated?
changeReason: Old contains content from the previous entry; this entry's JPN poses the question of whether the crime was spontaneous or premeditated, which new correctly translates.

TextENGNew: With no clear answers and no progress, the case took on all the hallmarks of going cold.
changeReason: Old includes content from the previous entry (the spontaneous/premeditated question); this entry's JPN describes the case becoming a cold case, which new correctly translates on its own.

TextENGNew: But...... years later.
changeReason: Old says 'the following year' but JPN 後年 means 'in later years' (an unspecified number of years later); new correctly translates as 'years later'.

TextENGNew: A new point of suspicion emerged regarding the note.
The two pieces of the note were not one half-page each from two separate B5 sheets...
Rather, they appeared to have originally been written on a single B5 page, with someone having torn out 'several lines from the middle' to delete them.
Judging from the letter size and the torn section, the deleted portion is estimated at two to three lines.
It is highly probable that the person who removed those lines was someone other than the suspect.
Additionally, the large quantity of adhesive tape residue found on the back of the clock gave rise to speculation that 'something other than the note had also been affixed there.'
The first person to discover the note was a detective long rumored to have connections to the case: Kuraudo Ooishi.
changeReason: Old says 'first discovered the crime' while in context 第一発見者 refers to discovering the note; new correctly specifies 'first person to discover the note.'

TextENGNew: He was voluntarily questioned but denied any involvement in the damage to the note...........
The following is the content of the note.
I, Keiichi Maebara, am being targeted for my life.
changeReason: Old renders 命を狙われています as 'am in fear for my life' (emotional state) while JPN means 'my life is being targeted' (active threat); new captures the active targeting.

TextENGNew: I do not know why, or by whom, my life is being targeted.
changeReason: Old omits 誰に ('by whom') from the Japanese; new correctly includes 'or by whom' which is an important element of Keiichi's statement about not knowing his attackers' identity.

TextENGNew: The only thing I do know is that it is connected to the curse of Oyashiro-sama.
Rena and Mion are among the perpetrators. There are at least four or five other adults. They possess a white van.
(End of the first sheet. The section below has been torn away horizontally.)
changeReason: New includes 真横に ('horizontally') describing how the note was torn, a forensic detail the old translation omits.

TextENGNew: (Beginning of the second sheet. The section above has been torn away horizontally.)
changeReason: Same as above: new includes the 真横に ('horizontally') detail about the tear direction that old omits.

TextENGNew: I have no idea why any of this happened.
If you are reading this, then I am probably already dead. ...Whether or not my body is found may be the only difference.
To whoever reads this. Please uncover the truth. That is my sole wish.@r Keiichi Maebara
